narrative: As a Hispanic male applying to law school, you've shared your undergraduate GPA of 3.30, an LSAT score of 36.50, upper-middle family income, and plans to attend full-time, along with your school deciles from Year 1 and Year 3. The admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try based on profiles like yours, estimated a 52.7% chance that you would fail it—crossing the threshold that led to your application's rejection. This prediction isn't a judgment on your potential or background, but a data-driven assessment comparing you to thousands of past students' outcomes. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2, which is notably lower than the typical student's 5.53 average and signaled to the model a potential challenge in sustaining strong performance leading into law school. Next in importance, your undergraduate GPA of 3.30 worked in your favor—above the dataset average of 3.21—pulling the prediction toward expecting bar exam success. Your Year 1 decile of 3, however, also below the average of 5.71, contributed toward the failure side, suggesting an early academic pattern that concerned the model despite your later strengths elsewhere. Compared to typical students who passed, your decile rankings placed you in the lower-performing groups, while your GPA aligned more closely with successful applicants. Your LSAT score, nearly matching the 36.70 average, and full-time status, shared by over 90% of students, had less sway here. This combination tipped the scales just enough for rejection, even as parts of your profile shone. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT or strengthening recent academic showings, perhaps through postgraduate courses or targeted bar prep programs that have helped similar students boost their profiles. Many in your position, including other Hispanic applicants, have succeeded by addressing these key areas and reapplying—your solid GPA and family support give you a strong foundation to build on. Keep pursuing your goals; this is one model's view, not your destiny.
